module.title: Sound Design II — Advanced
module.description: Go deep into FM synthesis, wavetable design, granular textures, and advanced modulation. Learn to build unique sounds from first principles and develop the synthesis vocabulary to create any timbre you can imagine.

lessons.title: FM Synthesis Deep-Dive
lessons.body:
FM (Frequency Modulation) synthesis produces sounds that no other synthesis method can. The metallic bells, glassy electric pianos, aggressive leads, and industrial noise textures that define electronic music from the 1980s to the present all have their roots in FM. Understanding FM at a fundamental level — not just turning preset knobs — gives you access to a vast timbral territory that most producers never explore.

## What FM Synthesis Actually Does

In FM synthesis, one oscillator (the **modulator**) modulates the frequency of another oscillator (the **carrier**). The carrier's pitch rapidly oscillates up and down at the modulator's frequency. This rapid frequency deviation creates **sidebands** — new frequencies that aren't present in either oscillator's original waveform.

The key insight: sidebands are what create the complex, inharmonic, metallic timbres of FM synthesis. The mathematical relationship between the carrier frequency and the modulator frequency determines whether the sidebands are harmonically related (producing musical, pitched sounds) or inharmonically related (producing bell-like, percussion-like, or noise-like sounds).

**Simple example**:
- Carrier at 440 Hz (A4)
- Modulator at 440 Hz (same frequency, ratio 1:1)
- Result: Adds odd and even harmonic sidebands at 880 Hz, 1320 Hz, etc.
- Sound: Bright, rich, more complex than a simple sawtooth

**Different example**:
- Carrier at 440 Hz
- Modulator at 660 Hz (ratio 2:3)
- Result: Inharmonic sidebands — some don't align with the harmonic series
- Sound: Bell-like, slightly metallic, not purely harmonic

## FM in Sytrus (FL Studio's FM Synthesiser)

Sytrus implements FM synthesis with up to 6 operators (oscillators), each of which can modulate or be modulated by others in configurable routing matrices.

**Operators**: Each operator has a waveform, frequency (ratio), and envelope. In typical FM synthesis, carrier operators are connected to the audio output, while modulator operators are connected only to the carrier(s) they modulate.

**The modulation matrix in Sytrus**: The central grid in Sytrus shows which operators modulate which. A check at row 1, column 2 means Operator 1 is modulated by Operator 2.

**Modulation depth (index)**: The amount of frequency deviation the modulator imposes on the carrier. Low index = subtle harmonic enrichment. High index = dramatic, bright, complex timbres. The modulation index is the most expressive FM parameter — it's essentially the FM equivalent of a filter cutoff.

## Building a Classic DX7-Style Electric Piano

The FM electric piano is one of the most recognizable sounds in music — used in pop, R&B, and jazz for 40 years.

**Operator structure (simplified)**:
- **Operator 1 (carrier)**: Sine wave → audio output. Controls the fundamental tone.
- **Operator 2 (modulator of OP1)**: Sine wave → modulates OP1 frequency. Envelope: fast attack and decay, zero sustain (the modulator fades quickly). This creates a bright transient that decays to a pure tone.

**Critical detail**: The electric piano sound comes from the modulator's envelope being fast and decaying. At the start of a note, the high modulation index creates a bright, inharmonic click (the "bell" of the piano). As the modulator envelope falls to zero, the modulation disappears and the carrier becomes a pure sine — the smooth, fading sustain of the piano.

**In Sytrus**:
1. Route OP2 to modulate OP1 (check in the modulation matrix)
2. OP1 envelope: slow decay, long release
3. OP2 envelope: fast attack, fast decay (fall to zero in 100–200 ms), zero sustain
4. OP2 frequency: Set to ratio 2.0 (one octave above OP1) — this ratio produces clean harmonic sidebands
5. OP2 modulation depth: 100–200 (experiment with higher values for brighter attack click)

## Building a Metallic Bell Sound

Bell sounds use inharmonic ratios:

1. OP1 (carrier): Sine wave → output
2. OP2 (modulator): Sine wave → modulates OP1, frequency ratio 3.5 or 7.0 (non-integer ratios create inharmonic sidebands)
3. OP2 modulation index: moderate (50–150)
4. Both envelopes: Long decay, no sustain (bells ring and fade)

The non-integer ratio of the modulator frequency is what creates the bell quality — the inharmonic partials are characteristic of metal being struck.

## FM Modulation Index as a Filter Equivalent

In FM synthesis, there is no traditional filter (low-pass, high-pass). Instead, the modulation index controls spectral brightness:

- **Low index (0–30)**: Few sidebands, dark tone, close to a sine wave
- **Medium index (50–100)**: More sidebands, richer harmonic content
- **High index (150–300)**: Many sidebands, bright, complex, potentially harsh

Automating the modulation index over time creates the FM equivalent of a filter sweep — the timbre brightens or darkens. This is a powerful automation technique that creates movement without changing any notes.

**In Sytrus**: Right-click any modulation depth knob → Create automation clip. Draw a curve to sweep the timbre in real time.

## Feedback FM

Some FM synths include a **feedback** path where an operator modulates itself. Self-modulation creates sawtooth-like waveforms (at moderate feedback) or noise (at high feedback). In Sytrus, enable feedback on an operator to add grit or distortion without an external effects plugin.

## FM for Percussion

FM synthesis is exceptional for synthesising metallic percussion — cymbal crashes, hi-hats, cowbells:

**Cymbal/hi-hat**: Multiple operators with inharmonic ratios (like 3.5, 5.7, 7.2) all feeding into one carrier at high modulation index. Short envelope (fast decay). The complex inharmonic result sounds like metal.

**FM kick**: Carrier sine wave with a steep downward pitch envelope. Modulator with fast-decaying envelope that adds click at the attack. The result is the FM equivalent of the 808 bass drum.
keyTakeaways: FM synthesis creates sidebands — new frequencies — whose harmonic or inharmonic relationship to the carrier determines whether the result sounds pitched and musical or bell-like and metallic.

The modulator envelope shape is as important as the carrier envelope: a fast-decaying modulator creates a bright transient that falls to a pure tone, which is the FM electric piano's signature character.
Automating the modulation index is FM's equivalent of a filter sweep — low index = dark, high index = bright — and creates evolving timbral movement without changing notes.
lessons.tryThis: In Sytrus, build the two-operator electric piano described above. Route OP2 to modulate OP1. Set OP2's envelope to fast attack and decay (under 200 ms), zero sustain. Set the ratio to 2.0. Play a chord and listen to the bright attack decaying into a smooth sine tone — that's the FM piano character. Now change the ratio to 3.5 — notice how the attack becomes more metallic and bell-like. Try ratio 7.0 and hear the bell become even more inharmonic. Write down how each ratio sounds different and why.

lessons.title: Wavetable Synthesis
lessons.body:
Wavetable synthesis is the dominant synthesis method in modern electronic music production. The characteristic morphing, evolving timbres of contemporary synths — Serum, Massive, Vital — are all based on wavetable technology. Understanding how wavetable synthesis works, and how to design sounds intentionally rather than browsing presets, puts you in command of one of the most expressive synthesis architectures available.

## What Is a Wavetable?

A wavetable is a collection of single-cycle waveforms stored in sequence. Each waveform in the table represents a different harmonic content — some are simple (sine, triangle, sawtooth), others are complex spectral snapshots of real sounds, carefully morphed synthesised waveforms, or custom-drawn shapes.

The oscillator plays a single waveform from the table at any given time. By moving through the wavetable — scanning from one waveform to another — the timbre changes continuously. This scanning motion is what creates the characteristic wavetable "morph" sound: a timbre that shifts and evolves even while sustaining a single note.

## Wavetable Position: The Core Parameter

The **wavetable position** (often called the scan position or WT pos) is the most important control in a wavetable synth. It determines which waveform in the table is currently playing.

- **Static position**: Lock the position to one waveform. The synth behaves like a regular oscillator with that specific waveform's harmonic content.
- **Envelope-controlled position**: The position moves automatically over the note's duration. A fast attack envelope on the WT position causes the timbre to morph quickly at the note start, then settle. This is how to get a wavetable "transient" that sounds different from the sustained tone.
- **LFO-controlled position**: The position oscillates back and forth continuously, creating a cyclic timbral modulation. Slow LFO rates create a gentle morph; fast rates create a tremolo-like effect.
- **Automation**: Draw the WT position manually in an automation clip for precise compositional control over when the timbre changes.

## Wavetable Synthesis in Harmor (FL Studio)

Harmor is FL Studio's additive/wavetable synthesiser. While it uses additive synthesis as its core engine, it can import images and audio as wavetable-style sources, and its partial control creates similar morphing behaviour.

For dedicated wavetable synthesis in FL Studio:
- **FLEX with wavetable presets**: FLEX includes presets that use wavetable-based oscillators — look for preset categories labelled "Wavetable" or "Digital"
- **Third-party**: Serum (Xfer Records), Vital (free), Massive (Native Instruments) are the industry-standard wavetable synths and work as VST plugins in FL Studio

## Designing Wavetable Sounds: Principles

Regardless of which wavetable synthesiser you use, the design principles are the same:

### Step 1: Choose the Wavetable

The wavetable defines the harmonic palette available to you. Categories:

- **Classic/analogue**: Wavetables derived from or emulating sawtooth, square, pulse waveforms. Familiar harmonic content, good for bass and leads.
- **Voice/formant**: Wavetables derived from human voice vowels. Scanning between them creates vowel-morphing effects — a "talking" quality.
- **Spectral**: Complex, inharmonic wavetables derived from analysed sounds. Useful for textures and evolving pads.
- **Distorted/dirty**: Heavily clipped or distorted waveforms. Aggressive and saturated.

### Step 2: Set the Wavetable Position

Decide if the position will be static (find the waveform in the table that sounds best for your purpose) or dynamic (use an envelope or LFO to morph through the table).

For pads: use slow LFO modulation on the WT position for continuous, subtle evolution.
For leads: use an envelope on the WT position so the attack sounds different from the sustain.
For bass: static position, choosing a waveform with strong mid-bass harmonics.

### Step 3: Unison and Detune

Most wavetable synths offer **unison** — multiple voices playing the same note simultaneously with slight pitch detuning. This creates the "supersaw" or "wall of sound" effect:

- **Voice count**: More voices = thicker, wider sound but higher CPU usage
- **Detune amount**: Small detuning (2–5 cents) = subtle width; larger detuning (10–20 cents) = obviously detuned, psychedelic width
- **Stereo spread**: Pan the unison voices across the stereo field for width

Unison on a lead synth: 2–4 voices, small detune, moderate spread. Creates a modern "fat" lead.
Unison on a pad: 8+ voices, moderate detune, full spread. Creates a lush, enveloping texture.

### Step 4: Filter

Wavetable synths typically include standard subtractive filters. The filter acts on the already-complex wavetable waveform:

- **Low-pass with resonance on a bright wavetable**: Creates that sweeping, resonant sound characteristic of modern electronic music
- **High-pass on a bass wavetable**: Removes sub content for a mid-bass-only texture
- **Band-pass with high resonance**: Creates a formant-like quality useful for vocals and leads

### Step 5: Modulation

The modulation matrix in a wavetable synth typically allows any parameter to be modulated by any source:

- **Velocity → WT position**: Harder-played notes use a different part of the wavetable, creating natural dynamics
- **Velocity → filter cutoff**: Harder hits are brighter
- **Mod wheel → detune amount**: Manual control over width
- **LFO → pan position**: Stereo tremolo from side to side

## Creating a Wavetable Pad

1. Choose a spectral or voice wavetable
2. Set unison to 8 voices, detune 12 cents, full stereo spread
3. LFO 1 → WT position, rate 0.2 Hz, depth moderate (25–40%)
4. Filter: low-pass at 3 kHz, resonance 0
5. Amplitude envelope: slow attack (400 ms), full sustain, slow release (800 ms)
6. Result: A wide, slowly morphing pad that evolves continuously without sounding mechanical

## Creating a Wavetable Lead

1. Choose an aggressive or distorted wavetable
2. Unison 2 voices, detune 4 cents, moderate spread
3. Amplitude envelope: fast attack (5 ms), short decay, medium sustain, medium release
4. Envelope 2 → WT position: envelope rises quickly from position 0 to position 50% over the first 200 ms of the note, then holds
5. Filter: low-pass at 4–5 kHz with envelope-controlled cutoff (opens fast, closes slowly)
6. Result: A lead with a distinct transient character that's different from its sustain — the defining quality of interesting wavetable leads
keyTakeaways: The wavetable position is the core expressive parameter — use an envelope to make the attack timbre different from the sustain, or an LFO for continuous morphing through the table.
Unison with detune creates the characteristic wavetable width: for pads, use 8+ voices at 10–20 cents detune; for leads, use 2–4 voices at 2–8 cents for a fat but focused sound.
Modulate the wavetable position with velocity (harder hits use a different part of the table) to create sounds with natural dynamics where playing intensity affects timbre, not just volume.
lessons.tryThis: Load a wavetable-based preset in FLEX or Serum/Vital if you have it. Find the wavetable position control (often labelled WT Pos or Scan). Move it manually across the table and listen to how the timbre changes. Assign an LFO to the WT position at a slow rate (0.2 Hz). Listen to the pad slowly morph. Then change the LFO rate to 2 Hz and listen to how the modulation becomes more of a timbral tremolo. Find the LFO rate where the sound feels most alive without sounding obviously modulated — that's your sweet spot.

lessons.title: Granular Synthesis and Textures
lessons.body:
Granular synthesis takes any audio source — a voice recording, a guitar note, a sound effect, white noise — and breaks it into tiny fragments called grains (typically 1–100 milliseconds). These grains are then played back, looped, overlapped, and rearranged in time. The result can range from smooth, ethereal textures to stuttering, glitchy, disintegrating soundscapes. Granular synthesis is uniquely suited to creating atmospheric, evolving sounds that are impossible to achieve with traditional oscillator-based synthesis.

## How Granular Synthesis Works

The granular engine has several key parameters:

**Position (scan position)**: Where in the source audio the grains are taken from. Locked at one position = static texture from that point in the audio. Moving across the source = revealing different spectral content.

**Grain size**: How long each individual grain is. Small grains (1–10 ms) create smooth, almost liquid textures — the grain boundaries are unperceivable. Large grains (50–100 ms) expose the individual grain boundaries, creating a more obvious "granular" texture.

**Grain density**: How many grains are playing simultaneously. High density = smooth, filled sound. Low density = sparse, stuttering, crackling texture.

**Pitch**: Each grain can be transposed. The pitch of the overall granular sound is set by which grains are selected and at what transposition they play.

**Position randomness**: Adding randomness to the grain position creates a shimmer or blur effect — grains from slightly different positions in the source are mixed together.

**Grain size randomness**: Random variation in grain size adds texture and prevents the "mechanical" sound of all grains being identical.

## Granular Synthesis in FL Studio

FL Studio's native granular tool is **Fruit Kick** (limited) and more usefully **Harmor**, which includes a resampling and image synthesis mode that can produce granular-like textures. However, for dedicated granular synthesis:

- **Grain** (third-party, free and paid): Purpose-built granular synthesiser available as a VST plugin in FL Studio
- **FL Studio Edison + Sample chopping**: A manual granular approach — record or import audio into Edison, select small regions, and trigger them at different pitches and rates
- **FLEX granular presets**: Some FLEX presets use granular engines — look for "Texture", "Atmosphere", or "Granular" categories

## Granular Techniques

### Granular Freeze (Time-Stretching)

Freeze the position at one point in a source audio file. The granular engine loops that instant indefinitely. Result: the harmonic content of that moment stretched into an infinite, sustained texture. This technique can turn a single guitar strum into an evolving pad, or a vowel sound into an ambient vocal texture.

### Granular Scrub

Slowly scan the position across the source audio over a long duration (8, 16, or 32 bars). This reveals the spectral evolution of the source — a recorded piano phrase gradually reveals each note's harmonic structure as it slowly scrolls past. The result is a pad that tells the story of the source recording in slow motion.

### Cloud Synthesis

Set position randomness high and density moderate. Grains are taken from random positions throughout the source, creating a diffuse cloud of the source's spectral content. A voice recording becomes a shimmery ambience. A string ensemble becomes an atmospheric shimmer.

### Granular Stutter

Set grain size to moderate (20–50 ms), density low, position randomness low. The result is an obvious rhythmic stuttering — each grain is audible as a discrete event. Synchronising the grain playback rate to the BPM creates rhythmically locked stutter effects.

## Creating Textures with Granular Processing

Granular synthesis is most powerful when processing existing audio:

**Process 1 — Ambient pad from a vocal sample**:
1. Record or import a vocal phrase into Edison
2. Load the audio into a granular engine (or use FL Studio's Harmor with an audio import)
3. Set position to a sustained vowel
4. Grain size: 30 ms. Density: high. Position randomness: moderate.
5. The vocal becomes an evolving vowel texture — recognisably human but ethereal

**Process 2 — Textural percussion from impact sounds**:
1. Take a drum hit (kick, snare, anything)
2. Set grain size very small (5 ms). Scan the position slowly.
3. The transient click and body of the drum are spread into a texture
4. Pitch it down 1–2 octaves for a sub rumble effect

**Process 3 — Metallic shimmer from a sustained instrument**:
1. Record or sample a sustained piano or guitar note
2. Freeze the position at the note's peak sustain
3. Add position randomness and slight grain size randomness
4. The shimmer of the instrument stretches into an ambient bed

## Granular in the Mix

Granular textures occupy a specific role in a mix: **atmospheric background**. They're almost never the foreground element. To mix granular textures:

- **Volume**: Low — -18 to -24 dB below the main elements. Felt more than heard consciously.
- **EQ**: High-pass at 200 Hz (remove any low-end mud), sometimes high-pass as high as 500 Hz for the lightest shimmer textures
- **Reverb**: Add a large hall or plate reverb. Granular textures and reverb are highly complementary — the reverb extends the grain cloud into the room.
- **Panning and stereo**: Granular textures should be wide. Use stereo widening or pan different grain streams slightly left and right.
- **Automation**: Automate the granular position or density over the arrangement — have the texture evolve with the song's structure. A texture that subtly changes every 8 bars maintains interest without demanding attention.
keyTakeaways: Granular synthesis breaks audio into tiny grains and reassembles them — freezing the position creates an infinite sustained texture from a single moment; scanning the position slowly reveals the source's spectral evolution.

Grain size is the primary texture control: small grains (1–10 ms) create smooth liquid textures, large grains (50–100 ms) create audible stuttering — choose based on whether you want the granular effect to be transparent or obvious.
Granular textures sit at -18 to -24 dB in the mix, high-passed above 200–500 Hz, processed with large reverb — they're felt as atmospheric ambience rather than heard as a foreground element.
lessons.tryThis: Find any audio recording — a piano note, a vocal phrase, ambient room noise. Import it into FL Studio's Edison. Select a 2-second segment and export it as a WAV sample. Load that sample into FL Studio's Sampler channel in loop mode. Set the loop to a very short section (about 50 ms) of the sample's sustain. Listen to the looping texture. Now slowly adjust the loop start point while it plays — this is a manual granular freeze that reveals how different parts of the sample sound when stretched into a sustained texture. Layer this at -20 dB under a melody and listen to the ambience it adds.

lessons.title: Advanced Modulation Routing
lessons.body:
Modulation is what separates static presets from living, breathing sounds. A sound that stays identical from the first millisecond to the last is less interesting than one that evolves, responds, and moves. Advanced modulation routing means using multiple modulation sources simultaneously, making sounds respond to performance input (velocity, pitch, modulation wheel), and creating complex interdependencies between parameters.

## Modulation Sources Available in FL Studio Synthesisers

| Source | Behaviour | Best Used For |
|---|---|---|
| Envelope | One-shot curve triggered by note-on | Transient shaping, filter sweep over note duration |
| LFO | Continuous periodic oscillation | Vibrato, tremolo, filter wobble |
| Velocity | Value set at note-on, varies by how hard | Expression, dynamics-dependent timbre |
| Pitch (note number) | Based on which note is played | Filter tracking, spectral shift with pitch |
| Mod wheel (CC1) | Continuous controller, set in real time | Performance expression, live filter/vibrato depth |
| After-touch | Pressure after the key is held | Sustained vibrato, brightness on held notes |
| Randomisation | Random value per note-on | Natural variation, making each note unique |

## Modulation Depth and Polarity

Every modulation routing has two properties:

**Depth**: How much the modulation source changes the target parameter. At zero depth, the modulation has no effect. At maximum depth, the target sweeps through its full range.

**Polarity (bipolar vs unipolar)**:
- **Bipolar** (−100 to +100): The modulation moves the target both above and below its base value. An LFO at bipolar modulating pitch creates vibrato that oscillates both sharp and flat.
- **Unipolar** (0 to +100): The modulation only moves the target in one direction from the base value. An envelope at unipolar modulating filter cutoff sweeps from closed upward — the filter never goes below the base cutoff.

In Sytrus, modulation depth for each mapping is set in the modulation matrix — positive values create positive modulation (the parameter moves up), negative values create inverse modulation (the parameter moves down when the source increases).

## Multiple LFOs: Building Complexity

A single LFO creates periodic motion. Multiple LFOs create complex, seemingly non-repetitive motion:

- **LFO 1 at 0.2 Hz**: Slow filter sweep cycling over 5 seconds
- **LFO 2 at 1.7 Hz**: Faster tremolo on the amplitude

Because 0.2 and 1.7 are not harmonically related, the two LFOs create a combined modulation pattern that repeats every 0.2 × 1.7 = 0.34 Hz... which means the pattern takes 2.94 seconds to fully cycle. In practice, they sound like they never repeat — creating organic, breathing movement.

**Tip**: Use LFO rates that are irrational multiples of each other (e.g., 0.15 Hz and 0.23 Hz). These create modulation that changes continuously without ever sounding mechanical.

## Envelope Chaining and Modulating Envelope Parameters

In some synthesisers, you can use one modulation source (like velocity) to modulate the shape of an envelope itself. Example:

- High velocity → faster filter envelope attack (bright notes hit harder)
- Low velocity → slower envelope attack (soft notes ease in)

This creates a natural dynamic response: loud notes are more percussive and bright; soft notes are more legato and warm. The effect mirrors the behaviour of real acoustic instruments.

## Velocity as an Expressive Tool

Velocity modulation separates played-sounding synthesis from robotic synthesis. Routing velocity to multiple parameters simultaneously:

1. **Velocity → amplitude**: Standard. Louder velocity = louder note.
2. **Velocity → filter cutoff**: Higher velocity = brighter timbre. Harder hits are brighter — just like a real piano or guitar.
3. **Velocity → modulation index (FM)**: Harder hits create more FM sidebands, a more complex sound.
4. **Velocity → wavetable position**: Different part of the table at different velocities.
5. **Velocity → envelope attack time**: Shorter attack on harder hits for more percussive response.

Using all five simultaneously creates a synthesiser that feels dramatically different at different velocities — the variation between a soft and hard note covers timbral, dynamic, and temporal dimensions simultaneously.

## Mod Wheel Performance Control

The mod wheel (MIDI CC1) is a real-time performance control. Routing it well transforms a preset into a performance instrument:

**Classic routing**: Mod wheel → LFO depth on pitch. At mod wheel = 0, no vibrato. At max, full vibrato. Standard on lead synths.

**Advanced routing**:
- Mod wheel → filter cutoff: Manual filter sweep in real time
- Mod wheel → wavetable position: Manually scan through the wavetable while playing
- Mod wheel → reverb send level: Add space while performing
- Mod wheel → detune amount: Build unison width as the performance builds

## Pitch Tracking Modulation

Modulating the filter cutoff with the note pitch (key tracking) makes the filter follow the played note:

- **100% key tracking**: The filter cutoff moves proportionally with the note. Playing an octave higher opens the filter by one octave. This maintains the same timbral character at all pitches.
- **Partial key tracking (50%)**: A compromise — the filter opens somewhat with higher notes but not fully proportionally. Low notes remain darker; high notes are brighter, but not excessively so.
- **Negative key tracking**: Lower notes are brighter, higher notes are darker — counterintuitive but creates interesting bass-heavy textures.

## Creating a Modulation Map Document

For complex patches with many modulation routings, it helps to write out the modulation map before programming:

| Source | Target | Depth | Polarity | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Env 1 | Amplitude | 100% | Unipolar | Main volume shape |
| Env 2 | Filter Cutoff | 70% | Unipolar | Filter sweep on note |
| LFO 1 (0.3 Hz) | WT Position | 25% | Bipolar | Slow pad morph |
| LFO 2 (4 Hz) | Amplitude | 10% | Bipolar | Subtle tremolo |
| Velocity | Filter Cutoff | 40% | Unipolar | Dynamic brightness |
| Mod Wheel | LFO 1 Depth | 100% | Unipolar | Manual morph control |

Documenting this before programming prevents losing track of what each routing does and helps you build logic into the patch design.
keyTakeaways: Multiple LFOs at irrational frequency ratios (e.g., 0.15 Hz and 0.23 Hz) create motion that never sounds repetitive — the combination cycles on timescales of minutes, producing organic, breathing movement.
Route velocity to 3–5 parameters simultaneously (amplitude, filter cutoff, envelope attack, FM index, WT position) so soft and loud notes feel dramatically different in timbre, not just volume.
Map the mod wheel to LFO depth or wavetable position for live expression control — this transforms a preset into a performance instrument that responds to real-time input.
lessons.tryThis: In Sytrus or any synth, build the following modulation map: LFO 1 at 0.3 Hz modulates the filter cutoff at 30% bipolar depth. LFO 2 at 1.8 Hz modulates the amplitude at 15% bipolar depth (subtle tremolo). Map velocity to filter cutoff at 50% unipolar depth. Play the same note at different velocities and hear the brightness change. Play a sustained note and listen to the two LFOs creating a movement pattern that never sounds exactly the same twice. Add a third parameter: velocity → envelope attack at -30% (harder notes have faster attack). Compare a very soft note vs a very hard note — they should feel like different sounds.
